segments.title: Source reliability and why citations matter
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke explains how relevance, reliability, and visible citations distinguish usable research from an attractive but unsuitable source.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke frames source choice as a practical research skill rather than a formality. His Terry Pratchett example shows that a source can be excellent for one question and unsuitable for another, so authority must be judged against the subject being investigated. Citations reveal both the tools a student used and whether the work moved beyond the assigned reading list into independent research. This is a useful starting point for evaluating evidence before building an academic or historical argument.

evidence.note: The transcript introduces the need for an appropriate amount of relevant information drawn from reliable sources.
The Terry Pratchett example contrasts a source useful within its fictional subject with one unsuitable for real-world engineering history.
Dr. Clarke says references show which tools the student used and whether independent research is taking place.

segments.title: Presentation polish affects how work is read
segments.summary: A practical aside explains why tidy alignment and paragraph structure can shape a marker's first response even when the underlying content is unchanged.
segments.body: This watch point addresses the human side of assessment. Dr. Clarke cites studies in which professionally aligned versions of the same work received slightly higher marks, then attributes the difference to ordinary marker psychology. Neat structure encourages a reader to look for credit, while clutter and weak paragraphing make omissions more conspicuous. Formatting cannot replace evidence or analysis, but it can make substantive work easier to read and reward.

evidence.note: The transcript describes studies in which more professionally aligned presentation changed marks for otherwise identical content.
Dr. Clarke contrasts the positive reading encouraged by a neat paper with the fault-finding response invited by untidy structure.

segments.title: Read prose aloud to expose weak style
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke recommends reading a draft aloud because hearing the sentences reveals awkward phrasing that silent reading often repairs unconsciously.
segments.body: The advice here is a simple editing test: read the work aloud. Dr. Clarke argues that silent reading tends to supply the wording the writer intended, which can conceal clumsy or broken prose. Hearing the sentences makes those faults much harder to overlook. Viewers can use this brief technique before submission to test whether an argument actually reads as clearly as it seemed while drafting.

evidence.note: The transcript says the brain can see what the writer intended during silent reading, whereas hearing the text exposes language that sounds wrong.

segments.title: Ethics can require more than best practice
segments.summary: A short caution separates accepted best practice from the ethically right action when procedure and judgment no longer align.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke distinguishes best practice, the accepted correct procedure, from ethics, the judgment about what is right. He says the two should ordinarily support each other. When accepted practice goes astray, however, the researcher still has to decide whether following it is ethically defensible. The passage is a compact reminder that methodological compliance does not remove personal responsibility.

evidence.note: The transcript defines ethics and best practice separately, says they should align, and advises choosing the ethically correct course when practice goes astray.

segments.title: Use corroboration to find consensus and outliers
segments.summary: Comparing several books and journal articles helps establish a consensus view while preserving dissenting sources for critical analysis.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke explains corroboration as an active comparison among multiple sources on similar subjects and from similar periods. Agreement supplies a defensible consensus and several references for the same point. Disagreement is not discarded, because outliers may contribute information that the majority view misses. The learning payoff is a clearer model of critical history writing: establish what sources share, then explain where and why they diverge.

evidence.note: The transcript recommends comparing several books and continuing to seek modern works and journal articles.
Dr. Clarke explains how agreement creates a consensus view and disagreement identifies outliers that support critical analysis.

segments.title: A polished answer still has to answer the question
segments.summary: The rocking-chair analogy shows why excellent research and prose cannot rescue an essay that answers a question the student invented.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke warns that a beautifully written and thoroughly researched essay can still earn a poor mark if it does not answer the assigned question. His analogy compares the mistake to a carpenter delivering six fine rocking chairs when a client ordered a dining table and chairs. Craft quality does not compensate for producing the wrong object. Opening the video here gives writers a memorable test for relevance before they invest further effort in research.

evidence.note: The transcript warns that strong writing and research cannot compensate for answering a question different from the assignment.
Dr. Clarke compares the error to delivering six rocking chairs when a six-seat dining table was requested.

segments.title: Build research from a rough plan and a key text
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke recommends drafting a rough plan, consulting a key text and subject librarian, then gathering further sources around a better plan.
segments.body: The proposed workflow begins before extensive source collection. Dr. Clarke advises making a rough plan, finding a key text, and asking a subject librarian or module guide for help locating appropriate material. Those first discoveries should improve the plan before the researcher gathers more sources. This sequence keeps reading tied to the assignment and makes the subject librarian part of the research strategy rather than a last resort.

evidence.note: The transcript recommends a rough plan, a key text, and help from the subject librarian or module guide.
Dr. Clarke says the key text and rough plan should produce a better plan before more sources are gathered.

segments.title: Balance research evidence with analysis
segments.summary: The marking example explains why both under-research and fact-heavy writing fail when the assignment rewards analysis more heavily than evidence alone.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke identifies two opposite research failures: too little information produces waffle, while indiscriminate collection produces a list of facts rather than an argument. He says a typical marking scheme may offer two or three analysis marks for every mark attached to evidence. A 3,000-word essay dominated by 2,500 words of new information therefore leaves too little room to earn the analysis marks. This watch point helps writers budget space for interpretation and critical thinking instead of assuming that research volume is the same as quality.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts insufficient research with essays overloaded by facts and says the required product is a thought-out analysis.
Dr. Clarke describes a rough evidence-to-analysis marking ratio and applies it to the word budget of a 3,000-word assignment.

segments.title: Use SEAL to keep paragraphs analytical and relevant
segments.summary: SEAL structures an academic paragraph as statement, evidence, analysis, and link while testing whether each point serves both the argument and the question.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke presents SEAL as the core structure of an academic paragraph: statement, evidence, analysis, and link. He asks writers to test how evidence relates both to their argument and to the assigned question. One paragraph where those relationships differ may contain productive thought, but several in succession signal that the essay has wandered away from its task. The final link should carry the reader into the next paragraph so the report or essay holds together as a continuous argument.

evidence.note: The transcript defines SEAL and asks how the evidence relates separately to the writer's argument and the assigned question.
Dr. Clarke uses consecutive paragraphs with different relationships as a warning that the essay is wandering off topic.
The passage returns to signposting, evidence, analysis, and links that make the whole essay flow.

segments.title: What a critical literature review demonstrates
segments.summary: A critical literature review demonstrates broad understanding, simultaneous consideration of multiple sources, and visible critical thinking.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke defines the critical literature review by the abilities it makes visible. Broad reading helps a researcher understand the topic and locate the new work within existing knowledge. The finished review must also show that the writer can hold and compare several sources at once rather than reporting them in isolation. Because markers can reward only what appears on the page, those acts of comparison and critical analysis must be expressed explicitly.

evidence.note: The transcript defines the review as evidence of broad understanding, engagement with multiple sources, and critical analysis.
Dr. Clarke stresses that comparing multiple pieces of information must be demonstrated on the page before it can be rewarded.

segments.title: A starter formula for organizing the review
segments.summary: The starter method divides the topic into key areas and compares a journal article, textbook, and web article within each section.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke offers this formula as a starting point rather than a rigid rule. For a roughly 3,000-word review, the researcher identifies key areas and finds a reputable journal article, textbook, and web article for each, adjusting the number of areas to the assignment length. Each section begins with the journal article, compares it with the textbook, contrasts both with the web source, and summarizes their relationship to the broader question. Repeating that pattern ensures that the draft compares different authors, publication years, and source types while the writer develops an individual style.

evidence.note: The transcript begins with a core text, turns the topic into key areas, and scales their number to the assignment length.
Dr. Clarke recommends a reputable journal article, textbook, and web article for each area and suggests asking a subject librarian to check uncertain web material.
The section gives the compare-and-contrast order for each area and presents it as a simple starting point from which writers develop their own style.

segments.title: Compare source types by reliability and currency
segments.summary: Journal articles, textbooks, and web articles carry different balances of peer review, editorial stability, novelty, and risk.
segments.body: The closing source comparison explains why the three-part formula produces useful tension rather than three interchangeable references. A reputable journal article tends to carry the strongest academic checking through peer review, though Dr. Clarke cautions that journal standards vary. A textbook has usually passed editing and library or departmental selection, giving it a measure of stability. A web article may contain the newest and most provocative information, but it has often received the fewest checks and therefore demands the most careful reliability assessment.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke says journal articles tend to be the most academically reliable because of peer review while noting that journals differ in quality.
The transcript describes editing and library or departmental selection as sources of textbook stability.
Web articles are described as potentially the newest and most interesting sources but also the least checked and possibly least reliable.
